title: Construction (Design and Management) Regulations 2015
chapters: SI 2015/51. 39 Regulations + 5 Schedules in 5 Parts. Made under HSWA 1974 ss.15, 80. Replaced CDM 2007 from 6 April 2015. Supported by HSE guidance L153 'Managing health and safety in construction'. CDM 2015 applies to ALL construction work — there is no minimum project value or duration threshold. Even small works such as office refurbishments, shop fit-outs, and building maintenance are within scope. The Regulations define FIVE duty holders: client, principal designer, principal contractor, designers, and contractors. An employer who commissions construction work is a CLIENT and has legal duties, regardless of whether construction is the employer's core business.

parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Made by the Secretary of State for Work and Pensions under the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Replaced CDM 2007 from 6 April 2015. Key changes: (1) replaced the CDM Coordinator role with the Principal Designer, (2) applied to ALL construction projects including domestic work (CDM 2007 exempted domestic clients), (3) simplified the notification threshold, (4) placed greater responsibility on the Client.

significantAmendments.effect: Introduced new duties for Principal Designers and Principal Contractors in relation to higher-risk buildings (buildings 18+ metres or 7+ storeys). HSE appointed as Building Safety Regulator. Gateway process for building control approval. CDM duties operate alongside the new building safety regime.

precedingDocuments.title: Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 (parent Act)
precedingDocuments.significance: CDM is made under HSWA 1974. Construction has consistently the highest fatality rate of any UK industry sector — more than 4 times the all-industry average. CDM exists because construction workers face exceptional risks that require a project management approach to safety, not just workplace-level controls.

precedingDocuments.title: CDM 2007 (SI 2007/320) — replaced
precedingDocuments.significance: The predecessor regulations. CDM 2015 simplified the framework, replaced the CDM Coordinator with the Principal Designer, and extended coverage to domestic clients.

leadingCases.name: Limited higher court case law — enforcement through HSE prosecution, often following fatalities
leadingCases.principle: CDM prosecutions typically follow serious injuries or fatalities on construction sites. The HSE prosecutes the duty holder who failed in their obligation — which may be the client, the principal designer, the principal contractor, or an individual contractor. Sentencing follows the Sentencing Council Health and Safety Offences Guidelines (2016). Fines for large organisations following a fatality can reach several million pounds. Directors may face personal prosecution under HSWA 1974 s.37 (consent or connivance of officer).
leadingCases.significance: The construction sector accounts for approximately 25% of all worker fatalities but only around 5% of the workforce. CDM exists because of this disproportionate death rate. An employer who commissions construction work and fails to discharge their CDM client duties may face prosecution if a worker dies or is seriously injured.

parts.title: Part 1 — The Hidden Obligation: Why Non-Construction Employers Are CDM Clients

sections.title: Scope, definitions, and why every employer who commissions building work has CDM duties
sections.summary: CDM 2015 applies to ALL construction work. 'Construction work' is defined broadly: building, alteration, fitting-out, commissioning, renovation, repair, upkeep, decoration, demolition, dismantling, structural maintenance, installation or removal of mechanical or electrical services. This means an employer who commissions an office refurbishment, a shop fit-out, warehouse shelving installation, car park resurfacing, roof repair, or even repainting is commissioning CONSTRUCTION WORK and becomes a CLIENT under CDM. The Client has legal duties — they cannot simply delegate everything to the contractor and wash their hands of safety.
sections.text:
WHAT IS 'CONSTRUCTION WORK' (Reg.2):
Includes (non-exhaustive):
(a) Building, alteration, conversion, fitting out, commissioning, renovation, repair, upkeep, redecoration, or other maintenance (including cleaning with water or an abrasive at high pressure, or with corrosive or toxic substances).
(b) Demolition or dismantling of a structure.
(c) Preparation for an intended structure, including site clearance, exploration, investigation (but not site survey), and excavation.
(d) Assembly or disassembly of prefabricated elements.
(e) Removal of a structure or any product or waste resulting from demolition.
(f) Installation, commissioning, maintenance, repair, or removal of mechanical, electrical, gas, compressed air, hydraulic, telecommunications, computer, or similar services normally fixed within or to a structure.

EXAMPLES OF NON-CONSTRUCTION EMPLOYERS WHO BECOME CDM CLIENTS:
— A law firm commissioning a refurbishment of its offices.
— A retailer fitting out a new shop unit.
— A manufacturer installing new production equipment (where the installation involves structural changes or fixed services).
— A hotel refurbishing guest rooms.
— A school extending its buildings.
— A hospital installing new medical equipment requiring structural or services modification.
— ANY employer commissioning roof repairs, window replacement, electrical rewiring, or plumbing works.

THE CLIENT'S DUTIES (Reg.4):
(a) Make suitable arrangements for managing the project, including allocating sufficient time and resources.
(b) Ensure that construction work is carried out without risks to health and safety, so far as is reasonably practicable.
(c) Provide pre-construction information to every designer and contractor.
(d) Appoint in writing a Principal Designer and a Principal Contractor (where more than one contractor is involved).
(e) Ensure that the Construction Phase Plan is drawn up before the construction phase begins.
(f) Ensure that a Health and Safety File is prepared and maintained, and provide it to anyone who acquires the structure.
(g) Where the project is NOTIFIABLE, notify the HSE using Form F10.

NOTIFICATION THRESHOLD (Reg.6):
A project is notifiable to the HSE if:
(a) The construction phase is planned to last more than 30 WORKING DAYS and have more than 20 WORKERS on site simultaneously at any point, OR
(b) The construction phase is planned to exceed 500 PERSON DAYS.
Notification does NOT change the duties — ALL projects have duties. Notification is an ADDITIONAL requirement for larger projects.

DOMESTIC CLIENTS (Reg.7):
CDM 2015 applies to domestic clients (individuals having work done on their home), but their duties are automatically transferred to the contractor (single contractor) or principal contractor (multiple contractors).
sections.keyPrinciple: The most common CDM failure is not knowing you are a CDM client. An employer who commissions an office refurbishment, a shop fit-out, or building maintenance IS a client and HAS duties. Ignorance of CDM status is not a defence. The HR manager should ensure that: (a) anyone in the organisation who commissions construction work understands the CDM client duties, (b) a Principal Designer and Principal Contractor are appointed in writing for multi-contractor projects, (c) pre-construction information is provided, and (d) a Construction Phase Plan exists before work starts.
refs: HSE L153 (Managing health and safety in construction — guidance)
Building Safety Act 2022 (additional duties for higher-risk buildings)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Managing health and safety in construction: CDM 2015 guidance (L153)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The primary HSE guidance on CDM 2015, covering all duty holders, the notification process, and practical compliance guidance.

commonErrors: Not realising the employer is a CDM client — ANY employer who commissions construction work (office refurbishment, shop fit-out, electrical rewiring, roof repairs) is a CLIENT with legal duties. This is the most common CDM failure
Assuming CDM only applies to large construction projects — there is NO minimum project value or duration. Even minor works like repainting or installing shelving can be construction work under CDM
Not appointing a Principal Designer and Principal Contractor in writing for multi-contractor projects — these appointments must be made IN WRITING. Verbal agreements are insufficient

Not ensuring the Construction Phase Plan exists before work starts — the plan must be prepared by the principal contractor (or contractor for single-contractor projects) BEFORE the construction phase begins. Starting work without a plan is a breach

Not providing pre-construction information to designers and contractors — the client has a duty to provide information about the existing site, including known hazards, previous surveys, and any restrictions

Not notifying the HSE for projects that exceed the thresholds — projects lasting >30 working days with >20 simultaneous workers, or >500 person days, must be notified using Form F10 before the construction phase begins

parts.title: Part 2 — The Five Duty Holders and Worker Protection

sections.title: Client, principal designer, principal contractor, designers, contractors — and the protection of workers on site
sections.summary: CDM 2015 distributes health and safety responsibilities across FIVE duty holders, each with specific duties appropriate to their role. The CLIENT initiates and funds the project. The PRINCIPAL DESIGNER coordinates health and safety during the pre-construction and design phases. The PRINCIPAL CONTRACTOR manages health and safety during the construction phase. DESIGNERS must eliminate or reduce foreseeable risks through their designs. CONTRACTORS must plan and manage their work safely. Additionally, Part 4 imposes specific requirements for construction sites: safe access, stability of structures, demolition procedures, excavation safety, prevention of drowning, traffic management, fire prevention, and emergency procedures. Workers have the right to be consulted, to receive site-specific induction training, and to raise safety concerns without fear of detriment.
sections.text:
THE FIVE DUTY HOLDERS:

(1) CLIENT (Reg.4): Initiates the project. Duties: suitable management arrangements, provide pre-construction information, appoint PD and PC in writing, ensure CPP exists, ensure H&S file prepared, notify HSE if notifiable.

(2) PRINCIPAL DESIGNER (Reg.11-12): Coordinates H&S in pre-construction and design. Duties: plan, manage, monitor pre-construction phase, identify and eliminate/reduce risks through design, ensure all designers comply, prepare the Health and Safety File, liaise with Principal Contractor.

(3) PRINCIPAL CONTRACTOR (Reg.12-14): Manages H&S during construction. Duties: plan, manage, monitor construction phase, draw up Construction Phase Plan, ensure cooperation between contractors, site inductions for all workers, consultation with workers, ensure site rules are followed, provide welfare facilities.

(4) DESIGNERS (Reg.9): Must eliminate hazards and reduce risks through design decisions. Must provide information about remaining significant risks to other duty holders. This applies to architects, engineers, interior designers, and anyone who makes design decisions affecting health and safety.

(5) CONTRACTORS (Reg.15): Must plan, manage, and monitor their own work. Comply with the CPP and site rules. Ensure workers have the necessary skills, knowledge, training, and instruction. Not begin work unless satisfied the client is aware of their duties.

WORKER PROTECTION (Part 4 + Reg.14):
(a) Workers must receive a site-specific induction before starting work on any construction site.
(b) Workers must be consulted on health and safety matters affecting them.
(c) Workers have the right to raise safety concerns without detriment (ERA 1996 s.44/s.100).
(d) Part 4 imposes specific construction site safety requirements: safe access and egress, prevention of falls from height (Work at Height Regulations 2005 also apply), stability of structures, demolition and dismantling, excavations, prevention of drowning, traffic routes and vehicles, fire detection and prevention, emergency routes and procedures, temperature and weather protection, lighting.
(e) WELFARE FACILITIES on construction sites: toilets, washing facilities, drinking water, changing rooms, rest areas — must be provided from DAY ONE of the construction phase. A contractor who starts work without welfare facilities is in breach.

SKILLS, KNOWLEDGE, AND EXPERIENCE:
All duty holders must ensure that those they appoint or engage have the skills, knowledge, experience, and (for organisations) organisational capability to carry out their role safely. This replaced the old CDM 2007 'competence' test with a more practical, proportionate assessment.
sections.keyPrinciple: CDM is a TEAM EFFORT — no single duty holder carries all the responsibility. But the CLIENT sets the tone. A client who appoints competent duty holders, provides good pre-construction information, allocates sufficient time and resources, and takes an active interest in safety will have safer projects. A client who squeezes timelines, appoints on price alone, and ignores safety will have dangerous projects. For the HR manager: if your organisation commissions construction work, the CLIENT duties fall on your organisation — not on the contractor.
refs: Work at Height Regulations 2005 (falls from height — supplementary)
ERA 1996 s.44/s.100 (worker protection — detriment and dismissal for raising H&S concerns)
commonErrors: Treating the contractor as the only person responsible for safety — the CLIENT, PRINCIPAL DESIGNER, and DESIGNERS all have duties. CDM is about managing safety through the ENTIRE project lifecycle, from design to construction to handover

Not appointing a Principal Designer for multi-contractor projects — the PD role is essential for coordinating health and safety during design and pre-construction. Without a PD, design risks are not managed
Starting construction before the Construction Phase Plan is in place — the CPP must be drawn up by the PC BEFORE the construction phase begins. It is not acceptable to 'do the paperwork later'

Not providing welfare facilities on day one — toilets, washing, and drinking water must be available from the FIRST DAY of construction. A contractor who starts work without welfare facilities is in immediate breach

Appointing on price alone without assessing skills, knowledge, and experience — the cheapest contractor may not have the capability to manage health and safety. The client has a duty to ensure those they appoint are capable

Not maintaining and handing over the Health and Safety File — the H&S file is a living document that must be maintained throughout the project and handed to the client at completion. It is then provided to anyone who later acquires the structure. A missing H&S file creates risks for future maintenance, refurbishment, and demolition

pendingAmendments.status: The BSA 2022 introduces new duties for Principal Designers and Principal Contractors for higher-risk buildings (18+ metres or 7+ storeys). HSE is the Building Safety Regulator. Gateway process phased in from 2023-2025. Monitor for further commencement orders.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — additional duties for larger construction projects
